<!-- Topic 4: Returning Data from a Function -->
<!-- Slides 39-48 -->

# Returning Data from a Function
<!-- Slide 39 -->

## Functions Can Send Data Back {.smaller}

+ Parameters move data into a function.
+ A return value sends one result back to the caller.
+ This completes the basic data-flow pattern for functions.

::: notes
Slides 39-48
:::

<!-- Slide 40 -->

---

## Return Type

```cpp
int add(int first, int second) {
    return first + second;
}
```

+ `int` is the return type.
+ It tells C++ what kind of value comes back.

<!-- Slide 41 -->

---

## The Return Statement

```cpp
return first + second;
```

+ `return` sends a value back to the caller.
+ The function ends when the return statement runs.
+ The returned value replaces the function call.

<!-- Slide 42 -->

---

## Store the Returned Value

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

int add(int first, int second);

int main() {
    int total = add(3, 4);
    cout << total << endl;
    return 0;
}

int add(int first, int second) {
    return first + second;
}
```

The variable `total` stores the value returned by `add()`.

<!-- Slide 43 -->

---

## Use the Returned Value Directly

```cpp
cout << add(3, 4) << endl;

int result = add(10, add(2, 3));
```

+ A returned value can be printed directly.
+ A returned value can be used inside a larger expression.

<!-- Slide 44 -->

---

## Return Type Must Match

```cpp
int getWholeNumber() {
    return 42;
}

string getName() {
    return "Maya";
}
```

The value after `return` must be compatible with the function's return type.

<!-- Slide 45 -->

---

## One Value Comes Back

+ A normal return statement sends one value back.
+ That value may be stored, printed, or used in another expression.
+ Later tools give us other ways to communicate more data.

<!-- Slide 46 -->

---

## Returned Data on the Stack

```{=html}
<div class="stack-demo" id="return-stack-demo">
  <div class="stack-controls">
    <button onclick="returnStackStep(0)">1. call</button>
    <button onclick="returnStackStep(1)">2. calculate</button>
    <button onclick="returnStackStep(2)">3. return value</button>
    <button onclick="returnStackStep(3)">4. continue</button>
  </div>
  <div class="stack-area">
    <div class="stack-label">Call stack</div>
    <div class="stack-frame frame-main">main()<br><span id="main-result">result = ?</span></div>
    <div class="stack-frame frame-helper" id="return-add">add(3, 4)<br>first = 3<br>second = 4</div>
  </div>
  <p id="return-stack-caption">main() calls add(3, 4).</p>
</div>

<script>
function returnStackStep(step) {
  const add = document.getElementById("return-add");
  const result = document.getElementById("main-result");
  const caption = document.getElementById("return-stack-caption");
  add.style.display = step <= 2 ? "block" : "none";
  add.classList.toggle("is-running", step === 1);
  result.textContent = step >= 3 ? "result = 7" : "result = ?";
  caption.textContent = [
    "The add() frame receives copies of both arguments.",
    "The function calculates first + second inside its stack frame.",
    "return sends 7 back to the caller.",
    "The add() frame is gone, and main() stores the returned value."
  ][step];
}
</script>
```

::: notes
[Graphic suggestion: show the return value leaving the function frame before the frame disappears.]
:::

<!-- Slide 47 -->

---

## Summary

+ A return value lets a function compute an answer.
+ The return type tells C++ what kind of answer comes back.
+ The caller decides what to do with the returned value.

<!-- Slide 48 -->
